text:
ADULT HEMATOLOGY / BONE MARROW TRANSPLANT PROGRAM
OUTPATIENT TRANSPLANT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Visit Type: Scheduled 6-Month Post-Allogeneic Transplant Surveillance Visit (Day +180)

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Ms. Renata Kowalczyk is a 49-year-old female with a history of de novo acute myeloid leukemia (FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN Adverse risk), status post 7+3 induction chemotherapy plus midostaurin, bridging consolidation with intermediate-dose cytarabine, and myeloablative allogeneic hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (10/10 MUD alloHSCT, FluBu4 conditioning, peripheral blood stem cell source) in first complete remission, now presenting for her day +180 scheduled surveillance visit. She returns today for disease status assessment, chronic GVHD monitoring, and ongoing survivorship management.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:
Ms. Kowalczyk is now approximately 6 months out from her matched unrelated donor allogeneic stem cell transplant. At the time of her day +100 visit, she was noted to have early manifestations of chronic GVHD affecting three organ systems: skin (lichenoid, reticulated erythematous patches over both forearms with lichenoid features, BSA <10%), oral mucosa (xerostomia and mild oral sensitivity), and eyes (dry eye syndrome, Schirmer test 7 mm/5 min), scored as mild chronic GVHD by NIH consensus criteria. Management at that time consisted of topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment to affected skin areas, clobetasol 0.05% ointment for more symptomatic skin areas, cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion (initiated by ophthalmology), preservative-free artificial tears, and Biotene oral rinse. Systemic immunosuppression with tacrolimus was continued and taper per protocol was initiated.

In the interval between the day +100 visit and today, the patient reports a progressive and gratifying improvement in her chronic GVHD manifestations. She notes that the skin lesions over her forearms have substantially faded — the erythematous, lichenoid patches have largely resolved and she is left with only faint areas of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation, which she finds cosmetically acceptable. She has been diligent with the topical tacrolimus application and sun avoidance measures. The oral dryness has also improved considerably; she no longer feels that her mouth is uncomfortably dry during the day and is managing well with Biotene rinse and adequate fluid intake. With respect to her eyes, she continues to use lubricating drops several times daily and the cyclosporine ophthalmic emulsion twice daily as prescribed by ophthalmology; she notes a meaningful improvement in eye comfort compared to the height of her dry eye symptoms, though she still has some residual dryness in low-humidity environments.

Systemically, she has been feeling substantially better over the past 6-8 weeks. She returned to her job as a high school biology teacher on a part-time basis approximately one month after her day +100 visit, and has been working full-time for the past 4-5 weeks. She reports this has been a tremendously positive development for her sense of identity and well-being. She denies new fevers, chills, night sweats, or unintentional weight loss. She has no new bleeding events, bruising, or petechiae. No gingival bleeding. Appetite is good. Bowel function is regular, with no diarrhea, hematochezia, or significant cramping. No new skin rashes or blistering. No jaundice or abdominal discomfort.

With respect to her prior transplant-related complications: The CMV viremia that had been detected around day +28 (460 IU/mL) responded well to valganciclovir; serial PCRs became undetectable by approximately day +70 and valganciclovir was discontinued after two consecutive negative results. No recurrence of CMV viremia has been detected on subsequent surveillance since discontinuation. The sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (mild, by Modified Seattle Criteria, diagnosed around day +12) resolved fully without defibrotide, and her liver function normalized by day +20. The severe mucositis she experienced during the immediate post-transplant period has fully resolved.

Of note, she was started on metformin for her type 2 diabetes at approximately day +90, after her renal function was confirmed to be stable. She checks her blood sugars at home intermittently and reports they have been running in a reasonable range. She has not required insulin. She also reports adequate vasomotor control with no significant hot flashes or sleep disturbance at this time (premature ovarian insufficiency is anticipated given her conditioning regimen exposure but formal endocrine workup is planned at the one-year visit per our protocol).

She was seen by ophthalmology approximately 6 weeks ago; the report noted improved Schirmer scores on the cyclosporine drops and recommended continuation of current eye care regimen. She was also seen by dermatology, who concurred with the management plan and noted near-complete resolution of the lichenoid skin involvement.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:
- Constitutional: No fevers, chills, or drenching night sweats. Mild residual fatigue but significantly improved from prior. No unintentional weight loss; patient reports stable weight.
- Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: Residual mild dry eye, well-managed on current eye drops. Mild xerostomia, improved. No oral ulcers or gingival bleeding. No vision changes beyond chronic dry eye symptoms.
- Cardiovascular: No chest pain, palpitations, or edema.
- Pulmonary: No dyspnea, cough, or wheezing. No shortness of breath at rest or with usual activity.
- Gastrointestinal: Appetite good. No nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, or abdominal pain. Regular bowel movements, no blood in stool.
- Genitourinary: No dysuria or hematuria. No urinary frequency changes. Menstrual cycles have not resumed, consistent with conditioning-induced ovarian suppression.
- Musculoskeletal: Mild bilateral hand tingling noted since the cytarabine consolidation cycle, mildly improved but still present; no weakness.
- Neurologic: No headaches, dizziness, or focal neurologic symptoms. The residual mild peripheral paresthesias in both hands are stable.
- Skin: As above; forearm skin lesions markedly improved. No new rashes, blistering, or erythema.
- Lymphatic: No new palpable lymphadenopathy noticed by patient.
- Endocrine: No polyuria, polydipsia, or new glycemic symptoms. No cold/heat intolerance. No hair loss beyond what has already occurred (now largely recovered).
- Psychiatric: Patient reports her mood is good. She is happy to be back at work and back with her family in a more functional capacity. No depression or anxiety symptoms reported.

---

MEDICATIONS (current at time of visit):
1. Tacrolimus (Prograf) oral — dose per transplant pharmacy, trough target now 5-8 ng/mL (actively tapering per post-transplant GVHD protocol)
2. Trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (Bactrim DS) — 1 tab three times weekly (PCP/Pneumocystis prophylaxis)
3. Acyclovir 400 mg twice daily (HSV/VZV prophylaxis while on immunosuppression)
4. Cyclosporine 0.05% ophthalmic emulsion (Restasis) — 1 drop in each eye twice daily
5. Preservative-free artificial tears (Systane Ultra preservative-free) — 4-6 times daily as needed
6. Lubricating ophthalmic gel (Refresh Celluvisc or equivalent) — at bedtime
7. Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment — applied to forearm skin lesions twice daily (now reduced to once daily given near-resolution)
8. Clobetasol 0.05% ointment — used intermittently on residual symptomatic skin areas as directed by dermatology; currently very infrequent use
9. Biotene oral rinse — twice daily
10. Metformin 500 mg twice daily (type 2 diabetes)
11. Calcium carbonate 600 mg + Vitamin D3 1000 IU — twice daily (bone health prophylaxis per transplant protocol)
12. Lisinopril 5 mg daily (hypertension)

ALLERGIES: No known drug allergies.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (relevant):
- AML, FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio (AR 0.82), DNMT3A R882H, ELN 2022 Adverse risk, de novo — as above
- Allogeneic HCT complications: mild SOS/VOD (resolved), febrile neutropenia, grade 3 mucositis, CMV viremia (resolved), mild chronic GVHD (resolving)
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus (managed with metformin)
- Mild hypertension (managed with lisinopril)
- Premature ovarian insufficiency (iatrogenic, secondary to myeloablative conditioning)

SOCIAL HISTORY:
The patient is a non-smoker. She lives with her husband and two adult children. She is a high school biology teacher and has returned to full-time employment. She denies alcohol use during transplant recovery per our counseling. She is highly engaged with her care and attends all follow-up appointments.

FAMILY HISTORY: No family history of AML, MDS, lymphoma, or other hematologic malignancies. No family history of solid organ cancers reported.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:
Vital Signs: Temperature 36.8°C, Blood pressure 124/78 mmHg, Heart rate 74 bpm (regular), Respiratory rate 16/min, O2 saturation 98% on room air, Weight 65.5 kg (baseline 67 kg; continued gradual recovery toward pre-illness weight).

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished woman in no acute distress. Alert and fully oriented. Appears younger than stated age. Pleasant and engaged in discussion.

Head/Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Hair regrowth noted — fine, early hair regrowth present; prior alopecia largely resolved. Oropharynx: Oral mucosa mildly dry in appearance but intact, no ulcers, no leukoplakia, no erythema. No oral candidiasis. Dentition intact. No cervical, submandibular, or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy. Thyroid: Non-palpable, non-tender.

Eyes: Mild conjunctival injection bilaterally, consistent with chronic dry eye. No discharge. No proptosis. Pupils equal and reactive.

Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezing, rhonchi, or rales. Good air movement throughout all lung fields.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema. No JVD.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Normal bowel sounds. No hepatomegaly (liver edge not palpable below the right costal margin). No splenomegaly (spleen not palpable). No masses. No ascites.

Skin: Forearms bilaterally show faint post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in areas corresponding to prior lichenoid eruption; no active erythema, no papules or plaques, no lichenoid features on current inspection. No new rashes elsewhere. No sclerotic changes. No active clobetasol-treated lesions visible today. Shins: No active GVHD lesions. No blistering anywhere. Skin is intact.

Neurologic: Alert, oriented x4. Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Strength 5/5 throughout upper and lower extremities. Mild decrease in light touch sensation in fingertips of both hands bilaterally (consistent with cytarabine-related peripheral neuropathy, mild). No cerebellar signs. Gait normal.

Extremities: No cyanosis or clubbing. No edema. PICC site (left arm, prior): Healed.

Lymph nodes: No palpable lymphadenopathy in cervical, axillary, or inguinal chains.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (obtained today, day +180):

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 6,200/microliter (reference 4,500-11,000)
- ANC: 4,100/microliter (reference >1,500)
- Hemoglobin: 12.6 g/dL (reference 12.0-16.0 for female)
- MCV: 91 fL (normal)
- Platelets: 176,000/microliter (reference 150,000-400,000)
- No circulating blasts on differential.

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 139 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L, Chloride: 104 mEq/L, Bicarbonate: 24 mEq/L
- BUN: 18 mg/dL, Creatinine: 1.12 mg/dL (eGFR ~61 mL/min/1.73m2, mild reduction, stable)
- Glucose: 112 mg/dL (fasting; mildly elevated, expected with tacrolimus)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal); Direct: 0.2 mg/dL
- AST: 22 U/L, ALT: 28 U/L (normal)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 74 U/L (normal)
- Albumin: 3.9 g/dL (normal)

Immunosuppression Drug Level:
- Tacrolimus trough: 6.1 ng/mL (within target range of 5-8 ng/mL for this phase of taper)

Infectious Surveillance:
- CMV PCR (quantitative, plasma): UNDETECTABLE — no evidence of CMV reactivation
- EBV PCR (quantitative, plasma): Undetectable

Additional Labs:
- HbA1c: 7.2% (acceptable glycemic control in the setting of tacrolimus-related insulin resistance; slight increase from 7.0% at prior visit, likely related to stable tacrolimus use)
- TSH: 2.1 mIU/L (normal)
- Magnesium: 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal; likely tacrolimus-related renal magnesium wasting; oral magnesium supplementation to be considered)

Chimerism (peripheral blood, sent at day +150, results available today):
- Whole blood: 100% donor chimerism by STR analysis
- No residual host hematopoiesis detected

Note: Bone marrow biopsy was performed at day +60 (results reviewed) and was MRD negative, morphologically normal, and confirmed complete donor engraftment (46,XY karyotype). The next scheduled bone marrow assessment is planned at the one-year post-transplant milestone (day +365).

---

IMAGING RESULTS (reviewed from recent studies):
Most recent imaging: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis (day +90) — reviewed. No extramedullary disease, no lymphadenopathy, no osseous lesions. Normal post-transplant findings. No new imaging was obtained at today's visit; no clinical indication to image beyond scheduled surveillance interval.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN:

1. AML, FAB M4, FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, ELN Adverse risk — STATUS: SUSTAINED COMPLETE REMISSION
Ms. Kowalczyk has now completed 6 months of post-transplant follow-up with no evidence of AML relapse. Her peripheral blood chimerism is 100% donor. The most recent bone marrow (day +60) was MRD negative by multiparameter flow cytometry, FLT3-ITD PCR was undetectable, and cytogenetics confirmed complete donor engraftment. Her CBC today is fully normal, with no circulating blasts. These findings are highly reassuring. We discussed this with the patient and her husband, who were clearly relieved. Given her high-risk disease profile (FLT3-ITD with high allelic ratio, ELN Adverse), achieving and maintaining MRD-negative status through transplant remains the overarching treatment goal, and she is meeting it.
- Plan: Continue current surveillance interval. Repeat bone marrow biopsy and chimerism analysis planned at day +365 (approximately 6 months from today). Peripheral blood chimerism to be checked again at approximately day +270. Surveillance CT chest/abdomen/pelvis will be performed around day +270 per our standard post-transplant imaging protocol.
- Patient instructed to return to the emergency department or call our urgent line for any fevers, new bleeding, extreme fatigue, or other concerning symptoms. Signs and symptoms of relapse reviewed (reappearance of gingival bleeding, new bruising, extreme fatigue, night sweats).

2. Chronic GVHD — mild, RESOLVING
By NIH consensus criteria, the patient's chronic GVHD was scored as mild at day +100 (skin score 1, mouth score 1, eye score 1). On today's exam, her skin lesions have largely resolved — only post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation remains without active lichenoid features. Oral symptoms have improved significantly. Eye symptoms are better controlled. No new organ involvement. Given this favorable trajectory, I am revising her NIH global severity score to mild-resolving, approaching the resolution threshold.
- Skin: Topical tacrolimus 0.1% ointment can now be reduced to every other day and used as needed for any return of active lesions. Clobetasol to be held unless active lichenoid re-flare. Continue sun protection and moisturization per dermatology.
- Eyes: Continue cyclosporine ophthalmic emulsion (Restasis) twice daily and preservative-free artificial tears as needed. Lubricating gel at bedtime. Ophthalmology to follow up at the one-year post-transplant visit unless symptoms worsen.
- Oral: Continue Biotene rinse. Dental cleaning recommended per our transplant protocol (high caries risk on immunosuppression with xerostomia); dental appointment to be arranged.
- Systemic: No escalation of systemic immunosuppression needed. No systemic corticosteroids warranted at this time given mild/resolving status.

3. Tacrolimus Taper — Ongoing
Tacrolimus trough today is 6.1 ng/mL, within the current target range of 5-8 ng/mL for this tapering phase. Given the patient's favorable cGVHD trajectory (improving, not worsening), we will proceed with the planned slow taper per protocol. Target: reduce dose by approximately 25% over the next 8 weeks, with the goal of reaching a trough of 3-5 ng/mL by day +270, and completing taper by approximately month 18 (day +540) if cGVHD remains under control.
- Monitor: Weekly tacrolimus troughs for the next 4 weeks following dose adjustment, then every 2-week troughs.
- Renal function: eGFR currently ~61 mL/min/1.73m2, mildly reduced from baseline (~72), likely reflecting tacrolimus nephrotoxicity. The modest decline is expected and acceptable at this juncture; continued improvement anticipated with dose reduction. Monitor creatinine and eGFR at each visit.
- Tacrolimus-related hypomagnesemia: Magnesium 1.8 mg/dL (low-normal). Starting magnesium oxide 400 mg twice daily by mouth with meals; recheck magnesium in 4 weeks.

4. Infectious Prophylaxis — Continued
Given continued tacrolimus immunosuppression, prophylaxis will be maintained:
- TMP-SMX (Bactrim DS) 1 tab three times weekly for Pneumocystis prophylaxis — continue.
- Acyclovir 400 mg twice daily for HSV/VZV prophylaxis — continue.
- CMV surveillance: CMV PCR today is undetectable (no recurrence since resolution of initial viremia at approximately day +70). Given patient is CMV seropositive with a CMV-seronegative donor (higher-risk D-/R+ pairing), monthly CMV PCR monitoring will continue for at least the first year post-transplant.
- No antifungal prophylaxis currently (was discontinued after neutrophil engraftment in the absence of ongoing high-dose steroids). Will re-initiate if systemic steroids become necessary for GVHD management.

5. Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus
HbA1c 7.2%, slightly up from 7.0% at the day +100 visit. Fasting glucose today 112 mg/dL. Worsening glycemic control is partially attributable to tacrolimus-mediated beta-cell toxicity and insulin resistance. Expected to improve as tacrolimus is tapered. Metformin currently at 500 mg twice daily.
- Given eGFR ~61 mL/min/1.73m2, metformin is acceptable but should be held if eGFR drops below 45. Will monitor closely with tacrolimus taper expected to improve renal function.
- Counseled patient on diet and exercise, importance of blood glucose monitoring at home. No insulin initiated at this time.
- Endocrinology referral to be offered at the one-year visit if glycemic control does not improve following tacrolimus taper.

6. Hypertension
Blood pressure well-controlled at 124/78 mmHg on lisinopril 5 mg daily. No medication changes needed.

7. Bone Health
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation in progress. DXA scan was obtained approximately one month ago (day +150 timepoint, results reviewed): Z-score -1.1, borderline low, consistent with steroid exposure and conditioning-related effects. No bisphosphonate therapy indicated at this Z-score; repeat DXA planned at 2 years post-transplant.
- Continue calcium carbonate 600 mg + vitamin D3 1000 IU twice daily.

8. Premature Ovarian Insufficiency (anticipated)
Patient has not had return of menses since myeloablative conditioning, consistent with chemotherapy/radiation-induced ovarian damage. FSH and AMH have not been formally checked yet — these are planned for the one-year (day +365) visit per our late effects protocol. Patient currently without significant vasomotor symptoms but endorses some residual mild hot flashes at night. Reproductive endocrinology referral to be coordinated for the one-year visit to discuss hormone replacement therapy (low-dose transdermal estradiol) if ovarian insufficiency confirmed.

9. Peripheral Neuropathy (residual, cytarabine-related)
Mild bilateral hand tingling/paresthesias persisting since cytarabine consolidation, now approximately 5 months out. Clinically stable, not worsening. No functional impairment. Likely cytarabine-related peripheral neuropathy. No specific intervention; monitoring. B12 level to be checked at next visit to exclude nutritional contribution.

10. Psychosocial
Ms. Kowalczyk has made a remarkable emotional and functional recovery. She is back to full-time teaching, which she finds deeply fulfilling. She and her family have been adjusting well to the surveillance phase of care. She was provided with information about the leukemia survivorship program, support groups, and mental health resources available through our cancer center. She expressed feeling optimistic and motivated. Will continue to screen at each visit using standard survivorship tools.

---

DISPOSITION:
The patient will follow up in transplant clinic in approximately 6 weeks for tacrolimus trough monitoring and to assess response to magnesium supplementation. Next major scheduled visit will be the day +270 (9-month post-transplant) visit, at which time surveillance CT imaging will be performed and chimerism will be reassessed. Repeat bone marrow biopsy remains planned for day +365.

She was counseled to call the BMT/AML nurse line immediately for any fever ≥38°C, new bleeding, or any concerns.

---

Time spent with patient: Approximately 55 minutes (25 minutes face-to-face, 30 minutes reviewing outside data and documentation).

Signed: Attending Hematologist / Bone Marrow Transplant Program
